coredata.dc:title: Copper- and iodine-doped nanozymes with simulated enzyme activity and efficient antifungal activity against Candida albicans

coredata.dc:description: Nanozymes are next-generation antifungal agents with enzyme-like characteristics and negligible biological toxicity owing to their ability to catalyze the production of reactive oxygen species. Herein, copper- and iodine-doped carbon dots (Cu/I-CDs) with intrinsic peroxidase-like antifungal activity are synthesized via a simple one-step hydrothermal approach. These Cu/I-CDs enhance the decomposition of H2O2 to generate •OH and efficiently kill Candida albicans. With an exogenous H2O2 concentration of only 0.5 mM, a low Cu/I-CDS concentration of just 0.585 mg/mL is required to achieve a 10% survival rate of C. albicans upon irradiation for 90 min with a light-emitting diode (16 W). The Cu/I-CD nanozyme effectively destroys C. albicans biofilms without causing significant toxicity both in vitro and in vivo. The prepared Cu/I-CDs accelerate the death of C. albicans in mouse wounds and vaginal models, thereby promoting wound healing and treating vulvovaginal candidiasis (VVC) in mice. The high biocompatibility of the Cu/I-CDs was verified by hemolysis and MTT assays. The Cu/I-CD nanozyme synthesized in this study shows great potential for application as an antifungal agent and may replace common existing antifungal drugs.

(a) Transmission electron microscopy images of Cu/I-CDs; (b) excitation and emission of Cu/I-CDs; X-ray photoelectron spectra of Cu/I-CDs: (c) C 1 s; (d) O 1 s; (e) N 1 s; (f) I 3d; (g) Cu 2p.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of ABTS, TMB, and OPD catalyzed by Cu/I-CDs + H2O2. (b) Steady-state kinetic curves of the Cu/I-CDs with TMB; (c) Lineweaver–Burk model of Cu/I-CDs for TMB; (d) and (e) temperature dependence of the TMB reaction system. pH dependence of the TMB reaction system.

(a) Antifungal effect of various concentrations of Cu/I-CDs with H2O2 (0.5 mM) on C. albicans under LED (16 W) and dark conditions for 90 min (BC: blank control, H2O2: H2O2-only, CDs + H2O2: different concentrations of Cu/I-CDs + H2O2). (b) Survival rate of C. albicans following incubation under LED irradiation (16 W) and dark conditions at different Cu/I-CD concentrations with H2O2 (0.5 mM) for 90 min (c) Time-dependent inhibition efficiency of the Cu/I-CDs (0.585 mg/mL) with H2O2 (0.5 mM) treated under LED irradiation (16 W) and dark conditions. (e) UV–vis absorption spectra of TMB with Cu/I-CDs (0.585 mg/mL) + H2O2 (5 mM) treated under LED (16 W) and dark conditions. CV staining assays to evaluate (f) anti-biofilm activity and (g) inhibitory activity of Cu/I-CDs against C. albicans biofilms.

Scanning electron microscopy images of C. albicans treated with 0.5 mM H2O2 or 0.585 mg/mL Cu/I-CDs under LED (16 W) irradiation for 90 min: (a) blank control; (b) treated with Cu/I-CDs; (c) treated with H2O2; and (d) treated with Cu/I-CDs (0.195 mg/mL) + H2O2; (e) Cu/I-CDs (0.38 mg/mL) + H2O2; (f) Cu/I-CDs (0.585 mg/mL) + H2O2.

Photographs of (a) C. albicans-infected wounds treated with different antifungal agents. (b) Changes in area of C. albicans-infected wounds after 0, 1, 3, 5, 7, and 9 d of treatment. (c) Body weight changes in mice infected by C. albicans during treatment on day 0, 1, 3, 5, 7, and 9. Effect of Cu/I-CDs on VVC in mouse model. Photographs of (d) agar plates and the corresponding mouse genital area; (e) survival rates of C. albicans treated with Cu/I-CDs (0.585 mg/mL) and H2O2 (0.5 mM).
Fluorescence images of C. albicans stained with S-CDs.
(a) Cell viability obtained from a cell counting Kit-8 assay and (b) hemolysis ratio of treatment with Cu/I-CDs.

(a) ROS generation by nanoparticles. Absorption spectra of AA after 3 h of treatment with Cu/I-CDs and H2O2 (100 mM) in PBS to an expected final particle volume of 3000 μL, after treatment with AA solution in PBS (to a final AA concentration of 5 mM), and after similar treatment of AA with PBS as the control. (b) Fluorescence spectra (Ex = 410 nm) of solutions of various compositions; (TA: 800 μL (5 mM); H2O2: 1000 μL (50 mM); Cu/I-CDs: 100 μL); (c) Histograms of ΔFL intensity spectra of hydroxybenzoic acid generated with LED light (16 W) or under dark conditions with Cu/I-CDs. (d) Absorption spectra of reaction systems (Cu/I-CDs + TMB + H2O2) with various scavengers under LED light (16 W).

head.ce:title: Copper- and iodine-doped nanozymes with simulated enzyme activity and efficient antifungal activity against Candida albicans

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanozymes are next-generation antifungal agents with enzyme-like characteristics and negligible biological toxicity owing to their ability to catalyze the production of reactive oxygen species. Herein, copper- and iodine-doped carbon dots (Cu/I-CDs) with intrinsic peroxidase-like antifungal activity are synthesized via a simple one-step hydrothermal approach. These Cu/I-CDs enhance the decomposition of H2O2 to generate •OH and efficiently kill Candida albicans. With an exogenous H2O2 concentration of only 0.5 mM, a low Cu/I-CDS concentration of just 0.585 mg/mL is required to achieve a 10% survival rate of C. albicans upon irradiation for 90 min with a light-emitting diode (16 W). The Cu/I-CD nanozyme effectively destroys C. albicans biofilms without causing significant toxicity both in vitro and in vivo. The prepared Cu/I-CDs accelerate the death of C. albicans in mouse wounds and vaginal models, thereby promoting wound healing and treating vulvovaginal candidiasis (VVC) in mice. The high biocompatibility of the Cu/I-CDs was verified by hemolysis and MTT assays. The Cu/I-CD nanozyme synthesized in this study shows great potential for application as an antifungal agent and may replace common existing antifungal drugs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Candida albicans (C. albicans) is the most significant pathogen contributing to the onset of vulvovaginal candidiasis (VVC). Four or more VVC infections within a year is defined as recurrent vulvovaginal yeast disease. Recurrent VVC (RVVC) is a chronic, widespread mucosal infection that is often drug-resistant, and has a huge negative impact on quality of life and work of patients [1–3]. Every year, RVVC has a serious impact on the reproductive health of an estimated 138 million women worldwide [4,5]. Guidelines from the Infectious Diseases Society of America recommend that patients with RVVC take oral fluconazole for at least six months [6]; however, under the current drug treatment, the disease has a high recurrence rate of over 60% [7,8]. The difficulty in treating RVVC stems from the formation of antifungal-resistant biofilms; thus, a new generation of antifungal drugs is urgently needed. The eukaryotic characteristics of fungal cells and the dual permeability of the fungal cell wall and membrane significantly hinder the development of new antifungal drugs [9–11]. Several classes of antifungal drugs with novel mechanisms are available for the treatment of these life-threatening infections (i.e., antimycotic-resistant C. albicans and recalcitrant biofilms). The term “nanozymes” was coined in 2004 [12], and the field has since developed rapidly, driven by the need to overcome the disadvantages associated with natural enzymes.

Natural enzymes have many disadvantages, including complex extraction processes and strict requirements regarding external storage conditions [13–15]. By contrast, nanozymes are inexpensive, easily produced, and widely applicable [16–18]. The peroxidase (POD)- and oxidase-like activities of nanozymes can catalyze the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) from H2O2 and O2, which are increasingly used in the fields of antibacterial agents, biosensors, and cancer treatment [19–21].

The nanozyme-catalyzed generation of ROS has emerged as a new strategy to combat pathogenic microorganisms [22,23]. The complex composition and structure of nano-enzymes combined with their relatively low catalytic activity leads to a low catalytic efficiency, which limits their practical application [24,25]. This low catalytic efficiency, in turn, leads to long reaction times and creates a requirement for higher than physiological concentrations of H2O2 [26]. In addition, complex and expensive synthetic procedures are required to synthesize nanozymes with high antifungal activity and selectivity [27,28]. Ideal antifungal drugs are characterized by convenient administration, rapid onset of action, a broad spectrum of activity, minimal side effects, and low cytotoxicity [29]. An antifungal drug with these properties can be developed via design and modification of nanozymes[30]. Currently, many metals and their compounds, such as copper[31] and iodine[32], are used in microbiology because of their potential antibacterial activity. In our previous study, in which beer-yeast–derived fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were used instead of conventional fluorescent dyes to label dead fungal cells, the beer-yeast–derived CDs could enter and stain dead cells of C. albicans [33]. Notably, an efficient copper-containing antifungal agent with excellent potential for clinical applications was used in this study.

Herein, we synthesized copper- and iodine-doped CD (Cu/I-CD) nanozymes with POD-like properties that exhibit high enzymatic activities. The antifungal activity against C. albicans is based on the production of superoxide (O2 −) and hydroxyl radicals (•OH). The Cu/I-CDs act as POD-like agents to catalyze the production of ROS, which provides a strong inhibitory effect on C. albicans in the wounds and vaginas of mice. The prepared Cu/I-CD nanozymes showed good biocompatibility in vivo and did not cause any adverse effects in the test subjects even after 60 d. Fig. 1.

Compounds 3-iodo-L-tyrosine (C9H10INO3), cupric chloride (CuCl2), o-phenylenediamine (OPD), and sodium hydrogen phosphate were purchased from Aladdin Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ethylenediamine, citric acid (C6H8O7), 2,2'-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS), epinephrine, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), isopropanol, 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-1-piperidinyloxy (TEMPOL), ascorbic acid (AA), and H2O2 solution (30%) were purchased from J&K Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Crystal violet (CV), and 3,3',5,5'-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) were purchased from Macklin Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

Culture medium RPMI-1640 was obtained from Zehao Science and Technology Co., Ltd. (Kunming, China). The human hepatocellular carcinoma (HepG2) cell line was purchased from Jinyuan Technology & Science Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Strains of C. albicans (ATCC90028) for experimental use were cultured at the Kunming University of Science and Technology. Sabouraud agar and dextrose broth were purchased from Beijing Land Bridge Science and Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China).

Cu/I-doped CDs were synthesized from cupric chloride and 3-iodo-L-tyrosine via a one-step hydrothermal method. 3-Iodo-L-tyrosine (0.4 g), CuCl2 (0.1 g), and ethylenediamine (100 μL) were separately diluted with ultrapure water (40 mL) to obtain three transparent and uniform solutions. The mixtures were heated at 180 °C for 8 h in a high-pressure Teflon vessel. The solution was subsequently allowed to cool to 20–25 °C and was then centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 20 min. The Cu/I-CD solution was filtered through a membrane (0.22 µm) to remove large particles; the obtained supernatant was refrigerated at 4–8 °C and used for subsequent experiments.

The as-prepared Cu/I-CDs were freeze-dried to obtain fine solid particles that were characterized using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (Bruker, Germany), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, FEI, USA), and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Kratos, UK). The enzymatic activity was measured by UV–vis spectroscopy (UV-2550, Hitachi, Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence spectra were recorded using a G9800A Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA). The morphological changes of C. albicans were investigated by field emission scanning electron microscopy (S-4800, Hitachi, Japan).

The POD-like enzyme activity of the Cu/I-CDs was evaluated in the presence of different substrates (TMB, ABTS, and OPD) and H2O2. A 100 μL aqueous solution of Cu/I-CDs was dispersed in a mixture of acetate buffer (0.2 mol/L HAc-NaAc, pH = 6.0) and H2O2 (2 mM, 400 μL), to which a solution of TMB (5 mM, 300 μL), ABTS (2.5 mM, 300 μL), OPD (10 mM, 300 μL), or H2O2 (50 mM, 100 μL) was added. The reaction was performed in a final volume of 4 mL for 20 min at 37 °C. The absorbances of the different mixtures of TMB, ABTS, and OPD were measured at wavelengths of 652, 420, and 415 nm, respectively. All experiments were performed in triplicate. The effects of the pH (from 2.0 to 8.0) and temperature (from 20° to 80°C) on the catalytic activity were also investigated.

The kinetics of the Cu/I-CD reaction were evaluated as a function of the TMB concentration. The maximum initial velocity (V
max ) and Michaelis–Menten constant (K
m ) were extracted from double reciprocal plots to quantitatively determine the catalytic efficiency of the Cu/I-CDs. The Cu/I-CD solution was dispersed in a mixture of acetate buffer (0.2 mol/L HAc-NaAc, pH = 6.0) and H2O2 (2 mM, 400 μL), to which a solution of TMB (0.1–0.6 mM) was added. The final volume of the reaction system was adjusted to 4 mL using the buffer solution. The absorbance of the mixture was measured continuously for 300 s. The kinetic parameters (K
m and V
max ) were then calculated using the Lineweaver–Burk plot:

All experimental apparatus was sterilized for 20 min at 121 °C before use to ensure that experiments were conducted under sterile conditions. C. albicans was maintained and propagated in Sabouraud dextrose broth. Thereafter, a single C. albicans colony was incubated in Sabouraud dextrose broth (5 mL) with shaking at 180 rpm and 35 °C for 24 h and diluted to approximately 1.0–1.5 × 106 colony forming units per milliliter (CFU/mL) for use in subsequent experiments.

The antifungal activity of the Cu/I-CDs was tested as follows. The fungal suspension (5 mL, 1.0–1.5 × 106 CFU/mL) was mixed with a certain concentration (0.195–0.585 mg/mL) of the Cu/I-CD solution for the photocatalytic experiment. The prepared samples, with and without Cu/I-CDs, were exposed to light from a 16 W light-emitting diode (LED) for 90 min. The control group was subjected to the same treatment under dark conditions. The as-prepared sample solution (100 μL) was then spread onto Sabouraud dextrose agar plates. After incubation at 35 °C for 48 h, the CFU of C. albicans were counted. The antifungal efficiency was then calculated using Eq. (2) [34]:
(2)

where N

sample corresponds to the number of colonies after treatment.

To prepare biofilms of C. albicans
, the strain was incubated in Sabouraud dextrose broth and diluted to approximately 1.0–1.5 × 106 CFU/mL for further experiments. Thereafter, the as-prepared fungal seeding solution (2 mL) was incubated at 37 °C for 24 h in 24-well microtiter plates. The biofilms were washed with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) before being collected from the bottom of the wells.

Cu/I-CDs (0.585 mg/mL) and H2O2 (0.5 mmol/L) were added to the wells of a microtiter plate. The prepared samples, with and without Cu/I-CDs, were exposed to light from a 16 W LED for 90 min. The control group was subjected to the same treatment under dark conditions. The collected biofilm was then incubated with this mixture in 1640 medium (1 mL) for 24 h at 37 °C, and subsequently rinsed with PBS (2 mL). CV staining was used to evaluate the destruction of the biofilm. The as-prepared CV stain (200 μL, 1% w/v) was added to each well of a microtiter plate and incubated at 37 °C for 20 min. Afterward, the wells were washed with PBS, and air-dried at 20–25 °C. Glacial acetic acid solution (1 mL) was added to each well and the samples were further incubated at 37 °C for 30 min to dissolve the CV. The mass of the remaining CV-stained biofilm was measured by measuring the OD of the suspension at 595 nm [35].

The inhibitory effect of the composites on biofilm formation was also studied. In a typical procedure, Cu/I-CDs (0.585 mg/mL) and H2O2 (0.5 mmol/L) were added to Sabouraud dextrose broth (1 mL) containing C. albicans (1.0–1.5 × 106 CFU/mL). The biofilm mass was measured using the CV staining assay after incubation at 37 °C for 24 h.

All animal procedures were approved by the Animal Ethics Committee of the Faculty of Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology and were performed in accordance with the Guidelines for Care and Use of Laboratory Animals. To investigate the antifungal efficacy of the Cu/I-CDs, female mice (20 ± 2 g, 6–8 weeks) were infected with C. albicans wounds as a model. After the mice were anesthetized, the hair on the back of the mice was shaved and the wounds were disinfected using 75% ethanol. After anesthesia, an 8 mm diameter wound was prepared on the spine of each mouse using a scalpel before a C. albicans suspension (50 μL, 1.0–1.5 × 106 CFU/mL) was dropped into the wound to establish the C. albicans infection model. After 24 h of C. albicans infection, the mice were randomly divided into four groups, and the wounds of each group were subjected to different treatments: PBS, H2O2, Cu/I-CDs, and Cu/I-CDs + H2O2. Each group contained at least nine mice. After infection for 24 h, 10 mM H2O2 solution (25 μL) and Cu/I-CDs (75 μL) were added dropwise onto the wound area of the mice in the corresponding groups, and the wound of each mouse was bound with gauze, which was changed at 24 h intervals. The solution of each group was exposed to LED light (16 W) for 90 min before administration. Each received 20 μL of their respective compound intravaginally daily for 7 d. A photograph of each wound was taken every 24 h, at which time the wound size was measured. The course of treatment was 9 d.

The mouse model of VVC was established according to the method published by Yano and Fidel [36]. Female BALB/C mice, aged 6–8 weeks, were kept under conditions of controlled lighting, aeration, and humidity, and were fed a standard diet, with water ad libitum. All treatments were performed on eight mice. Estradiol valerate (100 μL, Progynova 1 mg) diluted in olive oil (0.1 mg/mL) was injected subcutaneously once daily from 72 h before infection. On day 0, mice were inoculated vaginally with a C. albicans suspension (20 μL, 1.5 × 107 cells /mL), before undergoing an incubation period of 24 h to promote the growth of C. albicans and the colonization of the vaginal mucosa of the mice. At 48 h after inoculation, the vaginas of the mice were washed 10 times with PBS (20 μL). The rinse solution was then collected, mixed evenly, inoculated into SA plates containing chloramphenicol (50 μg/mL), and stored at 37 °C for 48 h to confirm infection.

The mice were divided into five groups (n = 8; all infected except group a), as follows: (a) non-infected PBS, (b) PBS, (c) H2O2 (0.5 mM), (d) Cu/I-CDs (0.585 mg/mL), and (e) H2O2 (0.5 mM) and Cu/I-CDs (0.585 mg/mL). The solution of each group was exposed to LED light (16 W) for 90 min before entering the body. Each received 20 μL of their respective compound intravaginally daily for 7 d. On the eighth day, approximately 100 μL of vaginal lavage fluid was collected to determine the vaginal fungal burden. After serial dilutions, the vaginal lavage fluid was spread on SA plates containing chloramphenicol (50 μg/mL) and stored at 37 °C for 48 h. Colonies were counted manually.

The quantified data were expressed as the standard deviation ± the mean. Statistical analyses were conducted using an independent-sample t-test. The differences in the data were considered significant at *p < 0.05, * *p < 0.01, and * **p < 0.001.

The C. albicans suspension (1 ×105 CFU/mL) was divided into four groups: control, H2O2-only, Cu/I-CD-only, and H2O2 + Cu/I-CDs. The concentration of H2O2 and Cu/I-CDs in the groups containing these compounds were 0.5 mM and 0.585 mg/mL, respectively. Fluorescence imaging was performed after incubation at 37 °C for 90 min under LED light (16 W). After centrifugation at 4000 rpm for 5 min, the cells were washed six times with sterile PBS (3 mL). The fungal suspension (20 μL) was placed on a slide and covered with a coverslip. The microscopic morphology of C. albicans was observed by confocal laser scanning microscopy.

HepG2 cells were cultured and maintained at 37 °C with 5% CO2 in RPMI-1640 supplemented with 10% FBS and streptomycin (100 μg/mL)/penicillin (100 U/mL). The viability of the HepG2 cells was assessed using a cell counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) assay. HepG2 cells were seeded at 4.75 × 103 cells/well in 96-well plates and treated with Cu/I-CDs (0.1–0.5 mg/mL). Cells were incubated with CCK-8 solution (10 μL/well) for 4–12 h. The optical density of each well was later measured with an ELISA reader at a wavelength of 450 nm.

Finally, a hemolysis test was performed to evaluate the biocompatibility of the prepared materials. Specifically, Cu/I-CD aqueous suspension (0.1–1.2 mg/mL), negative control PBS, and positive control 0.1% Triton X-100 were mixed thoroughly with rat red blood cells (RBCs) and incubated at 37 °C for 3 h. The absorbance of the supernatant was measured at a wavelength of 570 nm after centrifugation.

AA absorbs light at 266 nm. However, it can be oxidized by ROS to form dehydroascorbic acid [37]. A nanozyme dispersion (100 μL, Cu/I-CDs) to an expected final particle volume of 3000 μL and H2O2 (100 mM) AA solution (to a final AA concentration of 5 mM) were successively added to the HAc-NaAc buffer (0.2 mol/L, pH= 6.0). The final volume of the resulting dispersions was kept constant at 4 mL. The resulting dispersions were subsequently incubated at 37 °C for 3 h (LED, 16 W) before the mixture was analyzed via UV–vis absorption spectroscopy. The AA solution (5 mM) and H2O2 (100 mM) were used as controls.

Terephthalic acid (TA) was used as a fluorescent probe to detect •OH via the fluorescence intensity changes upon mixing with H2O2 and Cu/I-CDs. Specifically, TA solution (800 μL, 5 mM) was mixed with H2O2 (1000 μL, 50 mM) and Cu/I-CD dispersion (100 μL, 1.95 mg/mL) in phosphate buffer and incubated under LED irradiation (16 W) at 37 °C for 2 h. The final volume of the resulting dispersions was kept constant, at 4 mL. The fluorescence intensity at an emission wavelength of 410 nm was measured with a fluorescence photometer at an excitation wavelength of 305 nm. The fluorescence intensity of the emission is positively correlated with the concentration of •OH.

The important ROS were detected and identified by capture experiments. DMSO, TEMPOL, methyl alcohol, isopropanol, and tertiary butyl alcohol (TBA) were used as ROS scavengers. Samples containing different kinds of scavenger, (i.e. TMB + H2O2 + Cu/I-CDs), were analyzed by UV–vis spectroscopy. After incubation at 37 °C for 15 min (LED 16 W), the absorbance of the TMB mixtures was measured at a wavelength of 652 nm.

The structure of the synthesized nanospheres was observed by TEM ( Fig. 2a). The average diameter of the Cu/I-CDs ranged from 1.8 to 7.2 nm. Fig. 2b shows the optical absorption characteristic of the CDs. The as-prepared Cu/I-CDs showed fluorescence emission and excitation wavelengths of 502 and 384 nm, respectively.

The elemental composition of the Cu/I-CDs was further determined using XPS, which confirmed that the Cu/I-CDs consist of Cu, N, I, O, and C. The C 1 S spectra (Fig. 2c) shows peaks corresponding to C–N, C–C, and CO at binding energies of 286.2, 284.8, and 288.8 eV, respectively. The high-resolution N 1 s spectra (Fig. 2d) have peaks at 401.8 eV and 399.8 eV, which correspond to N–H and C–N, respectively [38]. Previous studies have demonstrated that nitrogen doping of carbon materials is essential to achieve high catalytic performance similar to that of enzymes. [39]. The O 1 s spectra (Fig. 2e) have signals corresponding to C–OH and C–O at binding energies of 533 eV and 531.5 eV, respectively. The two peaks located at 619.0 eV and 632.0 eV in the high-resolution I 3d spectra (Fig. 2f) may correspond to I 3d5/2 and I 3d3/2, respectively. These spectra show that further peaks at binding energies of 620.8 eV, 619.0 eV, 632.0 eV, and 628.0 eV, can arise from C–I and N–I moieties [40]. The Cu 2p spectrum has peaks which may correspond to Cu+ at 933.5 eV (2p1/2) and 932.7 eV (2p3/2) (Fig. 2g) [41].

Some Cu compounds are known to exhibit extremely strong POD-like activity [42,43]. Several of these are widely used for biocatalysis and bacteriostasis [44]. In these studies, TMB, OPD, and ABTS were prepared as substrates to verify the POD-like activity of the as-prepared materials. As shown in Fig. 3a, the color of the reaction system changed from colorless to blue, yellow, and green, and the absorbance values were maximal at 415, 420, and 652 nm, respectively. The POD-like activity of the Cu/I-CDs was verified by the increase in absorbance resulting from the presence of the oxidation products, TMB, OPD, and ABTS.

Various concentrations of TMB (0.1–0.6 mM) were mixed with H2O2 (2 mM) and Cu/I-CDs and the absorbance of the oxidized product was measured at 652 nm. The reaction kinetic parameters were obtained using the Michaelis–Menten model (Fig. 3 b, c), which afforded a K
m value of 0.33 mM and a V
max of 2.93 × 10−8 M/s for Cu/I-CDs with a TMB substrate. The K
m value of horseradish peroxidase (HRP) was 0.434 and the V
max value was 2.93 × 10−8 M/s with a TMB substrate in the same pH and temperature range [45]. And K
m value for Cu/I-CDs is significantly lower than that obtained using HRP, suggesting that the Cu/I-CDs exhibit a higher affinity toward the substrates than to HRP [46]. The peroxisase-like activity of Cu/I-CDs was effective at pH 4.0 ∼ 6.0 and temperature 30 ∼ 60 ℃. The K
m value for the Cu/I-CDs is significantly lower than that obtained using HRP, suggesting that the Cu/I-CDs exhibit a higher affinity toward the substrates than to HRP. According to the experimental results, Cu/I-CDs can exert its peroxisase-like activity in the range of normal body temperature and vaginal pH (Fig. 3b, c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro antifungal-activity assays of the Cu/I-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The antifungal activity of the Cu/I-CD nanozymes was investigated ( Fig. 4). In this study, the blank control group, H2O2-only, Cu/I-CDs-only, and Cu/I-CDs + H2O2 groups were tested. In the presence of only H2O2, the fungi had a survival rate higher than 80% regardless of the light conditions (Fig. 4a, b). The antifungal activity of the Cu/I-CDs-only group was investigated in the absence of H2O2, and the survival rate was higher than 60%, regardless of the light conditions or incubation in the dark (Fig. 4b). The addition of Cu/I-CDs significantly reduced the survival rate of C. albicans. In the presence of both Cu/I-CDs (0.585 mg/mL) and H2O2 (0.5 mM), the survival rate of C. albicans was less than 10% after 90 min of LED (16 W) treatment (Fig. 4b, c). The antifungal activity of the Cu/I-CDs + H2O2 increased with longer light exposure times (Fig. 4d). The H2O2 production of lactobacilli strains in the vagina of healthy women is known to be approximately 3.53 ± 0.15 mM [47]. Unfortunately, physiological concentrations of H2O2 were not sufficient to prevent the proliferation of opportunistic pathogens in the vagina. In contrast, an H2O2 concentration of 10 mM was able to effectively inhibit the growth of pathogenic bacteria in the vagina. Increasing the concentration of H2O2 beyond 10 mM increases the toxicity of probiotics in the vagina (i.e. L. vaginalis), thereby disrupting the vaginal microecology [48]. The Cu/I-CDs synthesized herein can strongly inhibit the growth of C. albicans at H2O2 concentrations lower than the physiological concentrations found in the vagina, thereby protecting the probiotics in the vagina as much as possible.

The antifungal activity of the Cu/I-CDs + H2O2 increased with increasing light exposure time. Samples of TMB mixed with Cu/I-CDs and H2O2 were incubated in LED or dark conditions, and the absorbance of the oxidized product was measured at 652 nm (Fig. 4e). The enhanced POD-like activity of the as-prepared Cu/I-CDs was confirmed by the increased absorbance by the oxidized products under light conditions. These results demonstrate that exposure to light increased the POD-like enzyme activity of the CDs, and subsequently increased their antifungal activity.

Fungal biofilms are complex colonies of microorganisms encased in a dense extracellular matrix of proteins, polysaccharides, and carbohydrates. Biofilms effectively protect fungi from both the host’s immune system and antifungal drugs [49,50]. The most common fungal biofilm found in humans is that of C. albicans, which is resistant to most antifungal agents such as polyenes and azoles [51]. The ability of existing commercial agents for VVC treatment to combat biofilms is limited; therefore, more effective therapeutic agents against C. albicans biofilms are required. After intermediate stages, finally reach the mature stage. In this study, we evaluated the anti-biofilm activity of the Cu/I-CDs, and calculated the biofilm inhibition efficiency (%) of each sample using Eq. (3) [52]:
(3)

Both H2O2 and the Cu/I-CDs alone caused little destruction of the biofilms of C. albicans relative to the control group (Fig. 4f). By contrast, the collaborative antifungal system containing Cu/I-CDs (0.585 mg/mL) and H2O2 (0.5 mmol/L) exhibited an anti-biofilm efficiency of 86.9% against C. albicans under LED irradiation (16 W) for 90 min C. albicans biofilm formation is a complex process. Recent research has shown that it begins when yeast cells attach to the surface of host tissues; the cells then undergo a metaphase, after which they eventually reach maturity [53]. The CV staining assay was also used to evaluate the ability of the samples to inhibit biofilm formation (Fig. 4f). Cu/I-CDs + H2O2 most effectively inhibited the formation of C. albicans biofilm formation, with a percentage inhibition of 75.71%. In this sense, the Cu/I-CDs + H2O2 group also shows promise as it has inhibitory activity, not only against biofilm formation, but also against mature biofilms (preformed biofilms; Fig. 4g). The mature C. albicans biofilm was less sensitive to Cu/I-CDs + H2O2 than the developing biofilm. This was consistent with other authors reporting that the MICs of compounds against C. albicans biofilms increase with biofilm maturation. Because established biofilm structures resist penetration by antifungal compounds the pounds against C. albicans biofilms increase with biofilm maturation [54,55].

Scanning electron microscopy was used to observe the morphological changes of free C. albicans that occurred during treatment with the antifungal system. The structure of C. albicans in the PBS treatment group (control group) was intact and the surface was continuous and smooth ( Fig. 5a). The C. albicans cells treated with Cu/I-CDs alone also showed this typical morphology, and the cell walls were intact and smooth (Fig. 5b). Treatment with H2O2 alone resulted in fungal cells with a rough surface (Fig. 5c); however, after co-treatment with Cu/I-CDs and H2O2, the fungal cells shriveled, and the cell wall was severely damaged (Fig. 5d, e, d). The above changes became more apparent with increasing Cu/I-CDs concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo wound healing performance of Cu/I-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Skin is one of the main barriers that protects the human body from pathogenic microorganisms, which can penetrate the skin through wounds and form colonies, leading to severe wound infection and even life-threatening complications [56]. We evaluated the healing effect of a low H2O2 concentration (0.5 mM) and Cu/I-CDs on infected wounds in a mouse model. The wound areas for the control group and the H2O2-only and Cu/I-CDs-only group were of similar sizes within the same time period ( Fig. 6a, b). On the ninth day of treatment, the wound areas of the control group, H2O2-only group, and Cu/I-CDs-only group were 52.77%, 43.28%, and 45.33% of the size of those before treatment, respectively. The H2O2-only and Cu/I-CDs-only groups exhibited little antifungal activity under dark conditions, which is consistent with the results of in vitro experiments. In contrast, the combination of Cu/I-CDs and H2O2 successfully promoted wound healing. The wounds treated with Cu/I-CDs + H2O2 were almost completely healed (11.33%), and were significantly smaller in area than the control and those treated with H2O2 or Cu/I-CDs. After 9 d, the wounds of mice treated with Cu/I-CDs + H2O2 were almost completely healed. No significant change in the body weights of the mice was observed during Cu/I-CD treatment, which indicates that the toxicity of the Cu/I-CDs nanozymes is low (Fig. 6c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of Cu/I-CDs in murine model of VVC
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the antifungal properties of Cu/I-CDs in the presence of H2O2, in vivo experiments in mice were performed. The mouse is a commonly used model for the development and study of numerous types of infectious fungal diseases. Because Candida species are not found in natural mouse vaginal microecosystems, it is essential to create a pathological state favoring vaginal fungal infection through hormones [57,58]. The estrogenization of mice is therefore essential to establish a mouse model of VVC. High estrogen levels are directly associated with vaginal epithelial thickening and elevated glycogen content, which mimics changes that occur during pregnancy or the menstrual cycle and thereby increases the susceptibility of the mouse vagina to C. albicans [36,59,60]. To maintain a persistent state of C. albicans infection in mice, estradiol was administered subcutaneously throughout the experiment, including before fungal inoculation [60,61].

The pseudoestrus state was established by subcutaneous injection of estrogen into female mice. A C. albicans suspension was then inoculated into the vagina to establish the VVC model. Fungal infection was confirmed by observing clinical signs in the infected animals (i.e., vulvar congestion, edema, and increased vaginal discharge) and C. albicans growth by culture of vaginal secretions (Fig. 6d) [62,63]. The same formulation (H2O2 0.5 mM within Cu/I-CDs 0.585 mg/mL) was used in the treatment of VVC in mice. Neither PBS nor H2O2 had any apparent effect on the fungal burden in the vagina of mice during the treatment period. As shown in Fig. 6e, the fungal load of Cu/I-CDs + H2O2 (11.91%) is lower than that of Cu/I-CDs (50.53%) and H2O2 (72.34%) after treatment. The redness and swelling of external genitalia of mice treated with Cu/I-CDs + H2O2 were significantly alleviated, and vaginal secretion was significantly reduced (Fig. 6d).

The ideal antifungal therapy must act on the fungus without affecting the host cells. Accordingly, the biocompatibility of the Cu/I-CDs was investigated to assess their potential biomedical applications [65]. Twenty-four hours after the sample treatment, the viability of the HepG2 cells remained at > 60%, even at high concentrations of Cu/I-CDs (up to 500 μg/mL, Fig. 8a). After refrigerating at 4–8 °C 60 days, no significant reduction in the enzymatic activity of the synthesized Cu/I-CDs was observed, and the sample retained its superior POD-like activity (Fig.S1). These phenomena demonstrate the extremely long-term stability of the Cu/I-CDs under physiological conditions.

The hemolysis assay method was used to confirm the biocompatibility of the synthetic Cu/I-CDs. The hemolysis of the Cu/I-CDs was assessed by measuring the UV absorbance of the reaction system in mouse erythrocytes at 570 nm after incubation (37 °C for 3 h). The absorbance of the supernatant at 570 nm shows that the hemolysis rate of the RBCs was the same as that of the PBS group, which remained lower than 5% even with Cu/I-CDs concentrations as high as 2.0 mg/mL (Fig. 8 b). These results demonstrate the Cu/I-CDs is non-toxic to HepG2 cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of antifungal activity of Cu/I-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The strong absorption of the antioxidant AA at 266 nm disappears upon oxidation [66]. POD-like active nanoparticles and metals could in principle catalyze the production of ROS bound to the metal surface [67]. The ability of the nanoparticles to mimic POD can be determined using AA as a probe to measure the production of ROS in the dispersion. Cu/I-CDs and AA were screened with the H2O2 for POD-like activities ( Fig. 9a). The most significant reduction in the absorbance of AA at 266 nm was observed using Cu/I-CDs after 3 h of co-incubation in PBS.

In the following experiments, TA was used as a substrate to verify the production of •OH. The fluorescence intensity of the emission is positively correlated with the concentration of •OH. When the Cu/I-CDs are appeared in the response system, the catalytic ta becomes hydroxyl terephthalic, which produces yellow fluorescence in the 410 nm. The fluorescence intensity was proportional to the concentration of hydroxybenzoic acid. The fluorescence intensity was significantly higher than that of TA alone or TA + H2O2 combined. This observation confirmed both the production of •OH and the POD-like nature of the Cu/I-CDs (Fig. 9b). When the other conditions remained constant, the fluorescence intensity was positively correlated with the Cu/I-CD concentration, confirming that the Cu/I-CDs catalyze the production of large amounts of •OH from H2O2. Meanwhile, the fluorescence of the Cu/I-CD + H2O2 mixture under LED (16 W) light was demonstrably higher than that under dark conditions (Fig. 9c), further demonstrating the photocatalytic-activity of Cu/I-CDs. Previous studies have confirmed that ROS (•OH, O2 −, H+) can damage bacterial cell membranes [68,69]; however, bacteria and fungi differ in terms of their composition, biological behavior, and drug sensitivity. We therefore designed the following experiments to test whether the catalytic activity of the nanozymes could promote the generation of ROS to inhibit the growth of C. albicans. Associated trapping experiments were performed to observe ROS by employing isopropanol and methyl alcohol as H+ scavengers [70], TBA and DMSO as •OH scavengers [71], and TEMPOL as an O2•− and 1O2 scavenger [72]. The absorbance of the mixtures was measured at a wavelength of 652 nm to identify any free radicals that may lead to antifungal activity. UV–vis spectra of the samples containing different scavengers, (TMB + H2O2 + Cu/I-CDs) demonstrated that the ROS scavengers reduce the UV–vis absorbance by varying degrees (Fig. 9d). However, the UV–vis absorbance of the sample decreased most significantly in the presence of DMSO, showing that the hydroxyl free radical played a key role in the reaction.

Photochemotherapy can achieve antimicrobial effects using photosensitizers (PSs) to generate ROS under light illumination. The PS is converted from the singlet ground state to a short-lived singlet state after photon absorption and subsequently forms a triplet excited state by intersystem crossover. Energy is then transferred to oxygen and water molecules to form ROS [73]. After irradiation with LED light, the production of ROS in the system increases significantly, thereby improving the antibacterial performance of the system significantly, which causes cell death by disrupting the integrity of the cell membrane [74]. The Cu/I-CDs constructed in this study have POD-like activity and can produce ROS. The POD-like activity of the CDs was used to convert low doses of H2O2 to •OH, thereby destroying the cell membrane and killing C. albicans. In this study, Cu/I-CDs were also used as a PS, and LED irradiation was used to increase the POD activity of the Cu/I-CDs and produce free radical oxygen to kill C. albicans. Considered together with the fluorescence imaging results, these results suggest that the damage to the membrane caused by ROS may be one of the death mechanisms of C. albicans.

A strategy for the synthesis of active Cu sites that exhibit high specific POD-like activity was presented. The synthesized Cu/I-CD nanozyme achieved a strong antifungal effect against C. albicans when combined with a low dose of H2O2. The inhibition rate of the synthesized nanozyme against C. albicans was greater than 90%, even with H2O2 concentrations lower than that found in the vagina. One potential mechanism of this antifungal action may be related to the formation of ROS, of which •OH plays a dominant role. The prepared Cu/I-CDs accelerated the death of C. albicans in mouse wounds and vaginal models, thereby promoting wound healing and treating VVC in mice. In addition, the prepared Cu/I-CDs showed strong potential to replace commonly used antifungal drugs owing to their low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility. This study has important implications for the development of new antimicrobial agents and the exploration of new antimicrobial strategies. In the future, we will explore the design, synthesis, and mechanisms of action of nanozymes with microbiocidal effects on fungi and other pathogenic microorganisms.
